"**Marcel**" **Cerdan** (French pronunciation: [[maʁsɛl sɛʁdɑ̃]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French"); 22 July 1916 – 28 October 1949)[[1]](#cite_note-1) was a French professional [boxer](/wiki/Boxing "Boxing") and world [middleweight](/wiki/Middleweight "Middleweight") champion in 1948-1949. He was considered by many boxing experts and fans to be France's greatest boxer, [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] and beyond to be one of the best to have learned his craft in Africa. Nicknamed "the Moroccan bomber" or "the man with hands of clay", [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] his talent led him to France in 1938 where he became French champion three times and then European champion in the welterweight category. His life was marked by his sporting achievements, social lifestyle and, ultimately, tragedy, being killed in [an airplane crash](/wiki/Air_France_Flight_009 "Air France Flight 009").

first_paragraph: **Marcellin** "**Marcel**" **Cerdan** (French pronunciation: [[maʁsɛl sɛʁdɑ̃]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French"); 22 July 1916 – 28 October 1949)[[1]](#cite_note-1) was a French professional [boxer](/wiki/Boxing "Boxing") and world [middleweight](/wiki/Middleweight "Middleweight") champion in 1948-1949. He was considered by many boxing experts and fans to be France's greatest boxer, [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] and beyond to be one of the best to have learned his craft in Africa. Nicknamed "the Moroccan bomber" or "the man with hands of clay", [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] his talent led him to France in 1938 where he became French champion three times and then European champion in the welterweight category. His life was marked by his sporting achievements, social lifestyle and, ultimately, tragedy, being killed in [an airplane crash](/wiki/Air_France_Flight_009 "Air France Flight 009").

**Marcellin** "**Marcel**" **Cerdan** (French pronunciation: [[maʁsɛl sɛʁdɑ̃]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French"); 22 July 1916 – 28 October 1949)[[1]](#cite_note-1) was a French professional [boxer](/wiki/Boxing "Boxing") and world [middleweight](/wiki/Middleweight "Middleweight") champion in 1948-1949. He was considered by many boxing experts and fans to be France's greatest boxer, [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] and beyond to be one of the best to have learned his craft in Africa. Nicknamed "the Moroccan bomber" or "the man with hands of clay", [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] his talent led him to France in 1938 where he became French champion three times and then European champion in the welterweight category. His life was marked by his sporting achievements, social lifestyle and, ultimately, tragedy, being killed in [an airplane crash](/wiki/Air_France_Flight_009 "Air France Flight 009").

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Marcel_Cerdan&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

Marcellin Cerdan was born at 9:00pm on 22 July 1916 in the "Little Paris" neighbourhood of [Sidi Bel Abbès](/wiki/Sidi_Bel_Abb%C3%A8s "Sidi Bel Abbès") in [Algeria](/wiki/French_Algeria "French Algeria"). He was the fourth son of Antonio Cerdán, a day labourer, and Asunción Cascales, *[pied-noirs](/wiki/Pied-Noir "Pied-Noir")* of [Spanish](/wiki/Spaniards "Spaniards") origin, with roots in [Alicante](/wiki/Alicante "Alicante").[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## Boxing career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Marcel_Cerdan&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Boxing career")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/Marcel_Cerdan_1969_Ajman_stamp.jpg/250px-Marcel_Cerdan_1969_Ajman_stamp.jpg)](/wiki/File:Marcel_Cerdan_1969_Ajman_stamp.jpg)

Marcel Cerdan on a 1969 Ajman stamp

Cerdan began boxing professionally on 4 November 1934 in Meknes, [Morocco](/wiki/Morocco "Morocco"), beating Marcel Bucchianeri by a decision in six rounds. He then ran a streak of 47 wins in a row between that first bout and 4 January 1939, when he lost for the first time, to Harry Craster by a disqualification in five rounds in [London](/wiki/London "London"). Cerdan campaigned heavily in the French territories of Algeria and Morocco during that part of his career, as well as in [metropolitan France](/wiki/Metropolitan_France "Metropolitan France"). In 1938, he beat Omar Kouidri in a 12-round decision at [Casablanca](/wiki/Casablanca "Casablanca") to claim the French welterweight title.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/Cerdan_Turiello_juin_1939.jpg/250px-Cerdan_Turiello_juin_1939.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cerdan_Turiello_juin_1939.jpg)

Marcel Cerdan (right) in front of [Saverio Turiello](/w/index.php?title=Saverio_Turiello&action=edit&redlink=1 "Saverio Turiello (page does not exist)") (left)

After his first loss, Cerdan recorded five consecutive wins, which led him to challenge Saviello Turiello for Europe's welterweight title in [Milan](/wiki/Milan "Milan"), [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy"). He won the European title by a decision in 15 rounds to continue his ascent towards the championship (back then, it was considered essential to own at least a Continental title belt to earn a world title shot; nowadays, it is not considered as important).

Cerdan's winning streak eventually reached 23 bouts before he suffered a defeat to Victor Buttin by disqualification for a second time, in eight rounds in [Algiers](/wiki/Algiers "Algiers") in 1942. They would later re-match in 1945, and Cerdan would avenge the defeat by knocking Buttin out in the third round.

For his next bout after the first fight with Buttin though, Cerdan put his title on the line against José Ferrer (namesake of the [Hollywood star](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Ferrer "José Ferrer")). He knocked out Ferrer in one round,[[4]](#cite_note-4) and won four more bouts in a row before facing another boxer with a namesake: James Toney, who shared that name with another [James Toney](/wiki/James_Toney "James Toney") who would become world Middleweight champion five decades later. Cerdan knocked out Toney in two rounds to keep this new winning streak alive. The new streak would reach 37 wins. In between, he joined the [American](/wiki/United_States "United States") allies in [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") during 1944, and he won the Inter-Allied Championship.

He also went up in weight to the Middleweight division, and won the French title by beating Assane Douf by a knockout in three rounds. He later claimed the vacant European title by beating Léon Foquet by a [knockout](/wiki/Knockout "Knockout") in one round. He retained that title a couple of times before losing it to rugged Belgian [Cyrille Delannoit](/wiki/Cyrille_Delannoit "Cyrille Delannoit") by a decision in 15 at [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels"), [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium"). Soon, he went back to Belgium and re-took the title by beating Delannoit, also by decision.

Finally, after the rematch with Delannoit, Cerdan was given a world title opportunity and he travelled to the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), where he beat World Middleweight champion [Tony Zale](/wiki/Tony_Zale "Tony Zale").[[5]](#cite_note-5) Cerdan became a world champion by knocking Zale out in the 12th round in Roosevelt Stadium, [Jersey City](/wiki/Jersey_City "Jersey City"), [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey") on 21 September 1948.

## Later years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Marcel_Cerdan&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Later years")]

During his short period as a world champion, Cerdan became a popular figure of the [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris") scene. Although married with three children, he had an affair with the singer [Édith Piaf](/wiki/%C3%89dith_Piaf "Édith Piaf"). The affair lasted from summer 1948 until his death in autumn 1949. They were very devoted to each other and Piaf dedicated one of her most famous songs, *[Hymne à l'amour](/wiki/Hymne_%C3%A0_l%27amour "Hymne à l'amour")*, to Cerdan.

For his first defense Cerdan returned to the United States, where he fought [Jake LaMotta](/wiki/Jake_LaMotta "Jake LaMotta") in [Detroit](/wiki/Detroit "Detroit"). Cerdan was knocked down in round one, his shoulder was dislocated, and he had to give up after the tenth round.[[6]](#cite_note-6) It would be the last fight of Cerdan's life. A contract was signed for a rematch and Cerdan went to training camp for it, but before camp began, he boarded a flight from Paris to New York to visit Piaf, who was performing there.

[The aircraft crashed into a mountain](/wiki/Air_France_Flight_009 "Air France Flight 009") on [São Miguel Island](/wiki/S%C3%A3o_Miguel_Island "São Miguel Island") in the Azores, while on approach to [Santa Maria](/wiki/Santa_Maria_Island "Santa Maria Island") for a scheduled stopover, killing all 48 people on board, including Cerdan and French violinist [Ginette Neveu](/wiki/Ginette_Neveu "Ginette Neveu").[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8) The following day, LaMotta lauded Cerdan's sportsmanship, saying: "You had to fight him as I did to know what a fine sportsman he was."[[9]](#cite_note-9) Cerdan was interred in the Cimetière du Sud, [Perpignan](/wiki/Perpignan "Perpignan"), [Pyrénées-Orientales](/wiki/Pyr%C3%A9n%C3%A9es-Orientales "Pyrénées-Orientales"), [France](/wiki/France "France").

## Legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Marcel_Cerdan&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Legacy")]

Cerdan's record was 110 wins and 4 losses two of which were disqualifications for low blows, with 65 wins by knockout.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

He is a member, along with LaMotta and Zale, of the [International Boxing Hall of Fame](/wiki/International_Boxing_Hall_of_Fame "International Boxing Hall of Fame").

In 1983, Cerdan and Piaf had their lives turned into a big screen biography by [Claude Lelouch](/wiki/Claude_Lelouch "Claude Lelouch"). The film, *[Édith et Marcel](/wiki/%C3%89dith_et_Marcel "Édith et Marcel")*, starred Marcel Cerdan Jr. in the role of his father and [Évelyne Bouix](/wiki/%C3%89velyne_Bouix "Évelyne Bouix") as Piaf. He is portrayed by actor [Jean-Pierre Martins](/wiki/Jean-Pierre_Martins "Jean-Pierre Martins") in the 2007 Piaf [biopic](/wiki/Biopic "Biopic") *[La Môme](/wiki/La_M%C3%B4me "La Môme")* (entitled *[La Vie en Rose](/wiki/La_Vie_En_Rose_(film) "La Vie En Rose (film)")* in English-speaking countries).

In 1991, a sports arena located in [Levallois-Perret](/wiki/Levallois-Perret "Levallois-Perret"), the [Palais des sports Marcel-Cerdan](/wiki/Palais_des_sports_Marcel_Cerdan "Palais des sports Marcel Cerdan"), was named in his honor.

## Titles in boxing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Marcel_Cerdan&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Titles in boxing")]

### Major world titles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Marcel_Cerdan&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Major world titles")]

* [NYSAC](/wiki/New_York_State_Athletic_Commission "New York State Athletic Commission") [middleweight](/wiki/Middleweight "Middleweight") champion (160 lbs)
* [NBA (WBA) middleweight champion](/wiki/List_of_WBA_world_champions#Middleweight "List of WBA world champions") (160 lbs)

### *The Ring* magazine titles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Marcel_Cerdan&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: The Ring magazine titles")]

* [*The Ring* middleweight champion](/wiki/List_of_The_Ring_world_champions#Middleweight "List of The Ring world champions") (160 lbs)

### Regional/International titles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Marcel_Cerdan&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Regional/International titles")]

* [European welterweight champion](/wiki/List_of_European_Boxing_Union_champions#Welterweight "List of European Boxing Union champions") (147 lbs)
* [European middleweight champion](/wiki/List_of_European_Boxing_Union_champions#Middleweight "List of European Boxing Union champions") (160 lbs) (2×)

### Undisputed titles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Marcel_Cerdan&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Undisputed titles")]

* [Undisputed middleweight champion](/wiki/List_of_undisputed_world_boxing_champions#Middleweight "List of undisputed world boxing champions")
